sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Diana Scott, a 100-year-old female, presented for a new patient consultation on 7/9/2021, primarily due to a recently diagnosed heart murmur.
She was referred by her previous doctor, who mentioned a new worrisome heart murmur.
She is concerned about her symptoms and is seeking further evaluation.
She reports severe leg pain for about a month, for which she is taking pain pills, and experiences shortness of breath when walking too fast or too long, or after drinking a full glass of water.
She also reports lightheadedness and dizziness, for which she takes meclizine.
Her symptoms have worsened over the past six months, with an estimated 70-80% increase in severity compared to six months ago.

Past medical history: The patient has a family history of heart problems, with her son having had a heart attack, her brother having had a stroke due to diabetes, and her eldest sister having had a heart problem.
She does not have a history of smoking, and there are no known allergies to medications.
Review of systems: The patient reports severe leg pain, shortness of breath, lightheadedness, and dizziness.
She denies fever, chills, or any other symptoms.
Current medications: Amlodipine, lisinopril, simvastatin (20mg), levothyroxine, and meclizine.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's physical exam revealed a normal cardiovascular examination, with a 4/6 systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border with mild radiation to the carotids.
Neurologic exam was normal for age.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a 4/6 systolic murmur at the upper right sternal border with mild radiation to the carotids, likely representative of aortic stenosis, which may be moderate.
Problem list: Cardiac murmur; suspected aortic stenosis; pvcs; hypolipidemia; lightheadedness/dizziness.
Plan:
The patient will undergo an echocardiogram to assess her cardiac structure and function, as well as the severity of her aortic stenosis.
Depending on the results, further evaluation, including an ischemic evaluation or a heart monitor, may be necessary.

She will continue to take her current medications, including amlodipine, lisinopril, and levothyroxine, and may be considered for a transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) or surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR), but this will depend on her overall medical history and the results of the echocardiogram.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in three to four weeks to discuss the results of the echocardiogram and next steps.
